title: Why Diffusion Models Don’t Memorize:  The Role of Implicit Dynamical Regularization in Training

reviews.summary: This paper investigates why diffusion models often avoid memorizing training data despite being highly overparameterized. The authors identify two distinct training timescales: an early τ_gen, at which high-quality, novel samples are produced, and a later τ_mem, at which memorization emerges; crucially, they find τ_gen to be approximately independent of the training set size n, while τ_mem grows roughly linearly with n, yielding a broadening “early-stopping” window for generalization as n increases. These claims are supported by experiments on CelebA with U-Nets across varying dataset sizes and capacities, and by an analytical study of a random-features score model in the high-dimensional limit that links the two timescales to distinct spectral bulks of a correlation matrix governing the learning dynamics.
strengths: The paper advances understanding of generalization in diffusion models by positing and substantiating an implicit dynamical regularization: a widening temporal gap between generalization and memorization as n grows.

Theoretical contribution: a tractable analysis of training dynamics in a random-features score model that derives the spectrum of the training operator and associates distinct spectral bulks with the two timescales. The formulation (Theorem 3.1) and asymptotic regime analysis (Theorem 3.2) are nontrivial and insightful.

The link between spectral bias, irregularity of empirical scores at low noise, and delayed convergence to memorization is conceptually appealing and potentially unifying across score-based approaches.
Systematic scaling studies across dataset sizes (n) and model widths (W), including rescaled-time collapses showing τ_mem ∝ n and τ_gen ∝ 1/W, lend strong empirical support.
Multiple complementary metrics: FID for sample quality, a nearest-neighbor-based memorization fraction, and training vs test losses at a fixed diffusion time capturing overfitting onset.
Controls in supplementary material (e.g., full-batch updates) help rule out sample-repetition artifacts as the driver of τ_mem.
The high-level picture is well motivated, with intuitive figures and a clear phase diagram distinguishing architectural vs dynamical regularization.
The theoretical exposition ties directly to observed empirical phenomena and highlights assumptions and regimes where results apply.
Addresses an important question—why and when diffusion models avoid memorization—relevant to both privacy and model reliability.
Offers practical guidance: early stopping within [τ_gen, τ_mem] and capacity control as reliable tools to avoid memorization without sacrificing quality.
The theoretical framework suggests generalization beyond diffusion to other score-based generative paradigms.
weaknesses: The theoretical model focuses on random features with fixed first-layer weights and a single diffusion time t, which is a simplification of practical, time-conditioned U-Nets; the extent to which multi-time coupling alters the spectrum is not fully explored.

Theorems rely on high-dimensional asymptotics and Gaussian/finite-covariance assumptions; while standard, they may miss important non-Gaussian or heavy-tailed structure common in real data (briefly noted as future work).
Theorem 3.2 is labeled “informal,” suggesting some steps are heuristic (e.g., replica method), which may limit the perceived rigor.
Experiments are conducted on 32×32 grayscale CelebA; broader validation on higher resolutions, color data, and diverse domains (e.g., CIFAR-10, LSUN, ImageNet subsets) would strengthen generality.

Memorization detection relies on L2 nearest-neighbor ratios; this can be brittle for faces on low-resolution grids. Complementary metrics (e.g., LPIPS-/CLIP-based retrieval, distribution coverage/precision–recall) would increase robustness.

FID for grayscale 32×32 can be noisy and may not be fully calibrated with the standard Inception features; additional quality metrics (e.g., KID or improved-precision/recall) would strengthen claims about τ_gen.
τ_gen and τ_mem are discussed and visualized, but operational definitions (e.g., precise thresholds for onset) are not fully formalized; quantitative sensitivity to these thresholds is not shown.

While the narrative is clear, key controls (e.g., full-batch vs SGD, Adam vs SGD) are deferred to the supplement; given their importance for ruling out confounders, a condensed main-text summary would help.
The connection between the “irregularity at low noise” and measured spectra or learned-frequency content in U-Nets remains qualitative; empirical frequency analyses would reinforce the story.

The paper cites and positions itself with respect to memorization literature and implicit regularization; however, a more direct empirical comparison to large-learning-rate regularization effects (e.g., [56]) and architectural bias results (e.g., [22, 23]) would contextualize the added value of dynamical regularization.
Additional discussion comparing to non-diffusion generative models (VAEs/GANs/flows) on early-stopping dynamics could broaden impact claims.
reviews.questions:
1. How exactly are τ_gen and τ_mem operationally defined across experiments (thresholds on FID improvement, f_mem onset, or loss divergence)? Could you report sensitivity analyses showing robustness of the scaling laws to these thresholds?
2. How robust are the empirical findings to alternative memorization detectors (e.g., LPIPS- or CLIP-based nearest-neighbor ratios, precision/recall with respect to training vs test sets)? Do you observe the same τ_mem ∝ n scaling?
3. Since you train a single U-Net conditioned on t, how does the choice of the time-conditioning scheme and the distribution over t during training affect τ_gen and τ_mem? For example, does upweighting small t alter the observed gap?
4. Can you provide a concise main-text figure (or quantitative summary) demonstrating τ_mem ∝ n under full-batch optimization? This would help rule out sample-repetition confounds more convincingly for readers who may skip the supplement.
5. Have you explored how learning rate affects τ_gen and τ_mem beyond the optimizer choice (SGD vs Adam)? Is the n-scaling preserved under large learning rates where implicit regularization [56] is strongest?
6. Do your conclusions hold at higher image resolutions and for color images? Any preliminary results on CIFAR-10 or a higher-res CelebA variant would greatly enhance generality.
7. In the RFNN theory, could you comment on extensions to multi-time training (integrated t) and to learned first-layer features? Do you expect the two-bulk spectral separation—and thus the two-timescale phenomenon—to persist?
reviews.assessment:
This paper presents a compelling and well-substantiated account of why diffusion models frequently avoid memorization: the training dynamics exhibit a robust separation between an early generalization time and a much later memorization time that expands with dataset size, enabling a broad early-stopping window. The experimental evidence—though somewhat limited in dataset diversity and resolution—is systematic and reinforced by clean scaling collapses. The theoretical analysis via random features is technically solid and yields an interpretable spectral mechanism for the two timescales, complementing prior asymptotic (τ → ∞) studies. The paper is clearly written and connects well to relevant prior work, while identifying and motivating future directions.

The main weaknesses concern experimental scope (grayscale 32×32 CelebA only), reliance on FID and L2-NN memorization metrics, and the simplifications in the theoretical model (fixed t, random features). These limitations are acknowledged and do not, in my view, undermine the central contributions. I recommend acceptance. The work offers meaningful theoretical and empirical advances on an important question, with practical implications for training safer generative models.
